original_abstract: Although the last few decades have witnessed a shift from conscription to all-volunteer forces in many European countries, recent changes in the European security environment have raised new concerns and prompted several countries to reconsider conscription. However, modern conscription systems have also had to adapt to changing societies and an increasing emphasis on individualistic and neoliberal values. This study aims to examine the various forms of military conscription and illuminate the continuing viability of the conscription model in six European countries: Estonia, Finland, Lithuania, Norway, Sweden, and Switzerland. Four of these countries have consistently maintained conscription (Estonia, Finland, Norway, and Switzerland), while Lithuania and Sweden have recently reinstated conscription. Although conscription systems differ among these countries, there is overall an increasing emphasis on conscript motivation and female participation. Three key aspects are identified to describe different conscription systems: the proportion of conscripted individuals in the birth cohort, motivation to serve, and the approach to female conscripts. Combining these aspects, the study identifies three types of conscription system in these six countries. This article demonstrates that conscription has evolved and adapted to changing societies and security situations instead of merely retaining its traditional form.
humanized_abstract: In the last few decades, almost all European countries with conscript forces have switched to either voluntary or contract-based all-volunteer forces. However, new challenges facing European security have sparked conversations on conscription in some countries. At the same time, modern conscript forces are operating in an increasingly individualistic and neoliberal society. This article examines different forms of conscription and its contemporary relevance and vitality in the context of six European countries: Estonia, Finland, Lithuania, Norway, Sweden and Switzerland. While four of the countries have maintained conscription, and continue to do so, Lithuania and Sweden reintroduced conscription recently. The conscription systems in the six countries diverge, and the dominant focus on conscript motivation and women in the military is evolving. The core characteristics that describe conscript forces include three elements: the conscription rate (i.e. the number of conscripts as a proportion of each birth cohort), conscript motivation and the way women are incorporated into conscript forces. By combining the characteristics of conscription systems, we can classify them into three categories, and argue more generally that conscription is a dynamic institution that has adapted to new societal demands and to changing circumstances, rather than standing still at the traditional status quo.
